body: Welcome to the CU Boulder Data Science Community – your virtual campus hub! Whether you're studying in your living room, a local coffee shop, or halfway across the world, this space is designed just for you - CU Boulder Data Science students - to connect, collaborate, and thrive together. As moderators, we’ll drop in occasionally with info and encouragement – but this space is really about you connecting with each other. Watch below for a short walkthrough of the platform. Some considerations as you engage with our community: Collaborate: Help your peers! Do you know the answer? Jump in! Education is collaborative. Be respectful : Always communicate with respect and kindness, and be open to diverse viewpoints. Look around: As you begin to ask a question, posts or resources in the community will be suggested to you. Our goal is to get you to the correct information as quickly as possible. Be Constructive : Whether you're agreeing, disagreeing, or providing feedback, aim to be constructive and provide value to the discussion. Be Open to Feedback : If someone corrects or disagrees with you, approach the feedback with an open mind. It's an opportunity to learn. Follow the Honor Code: Please review the CU Boulder honor code and guidelines to help maintain a respectful learning environment. We look forward to building a supportive and impactful learning community together. Go Buffs! This community is built using InScribe. If you have questions or need product support, visit the InScribe Support Community .

title: Job hunting feels like shouting into a void. A new Stanford study suggests the void is just one vendor.
body: A new Stanford study (4M applications, 3M applicants, all screened by one vendor) found that around 10% of people who applied to four positions got rejected by all of them, at a rate far higher than you’d expect if firms were deciding independently. The cause: a small number of models doing the screening. They counted 42 across firms. The unsettling detail — applicants never submitted name, race, gender, or age. The screening was behavioral (gamified tasks). Clear racial disparities still showed up, because behavior itself encodes background. And the vendor’s own published audits showed no measurable bias, because they aggregated across all positions. Disaggregate position-by-position (which is how the law actually evaluates discrimination) and the disparity shows. Two questions for this group: 1. If a chunk of “remote” rejections are correlated rather than independent, mass-applying through identical pipelines does nothing. What’s actually working for you to get a different evaluation: referrals, niche boards, direct outreach, project work? 2. The study’s advice is “apply widely, lean on weak ties, build demonstrable proof.” Is that enough, or is it just telling us to run harder on a treadmill? Link: https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/news/qa-algorithmic-monoculture/

body: Volos train statiom I have painted Hello everyone! I hope you are havin a successful session so far. In the summer months, I sometimes replace walking with cycling. I find physical activity great both for my physical and mental fitness, so I don't skip it. On my journeys, I photograph a lot and I am keeping a collection of photographs of each trip I made. As souvenirs, I bring home cans of local beers. I have collected around 500 different beer cans from different countries so far! The most unusual hobby I have is painting. I paint on canvas using acrylic paints. title: 2026 Science Education and Internship Program (SEIP) fellowship
body: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Oak Ridge Office of Environmental Management (OREM) is now accepting applications for its 2026 Science Education and Internship Program (SEIP) fellowships. OREM leads one of the nation’s largest and most complex environmental cleanup and revitalization efforts. Its mission includes: Removing Manhattan Project- and Cold War-era infrastructure at the Y‑12 National Security Complex and Oak Ridge National Laboratory Addressing legacy nuclear materials and environmental impacts Modernizing critical national security and research sites Returning remediated land to the community to support new economic development OREM carries out this mission using strong project management practices, sound business operations, regulatory compliance, and innovative science and engineering solutions. Fellows learn alongside federal professionals and subject matter experts on active, real-world projects that directly support this national mission. Appointment durations are 2 years (with possible extension). Business & Contracting Fellow Reference Code: DOE-OREM-SEIP-2026-BCF Ideal for students or recent graduates in: Business Accounting & Finance Computer, Information, & Data Sciences Mathematics/Statistics Risk Analysis Fellows gain experience in: Federal contracting and procurement Project budgeting and scheduling Project controls and quality assurance Real estate and property management Organizational performance and business operations General Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Be a U.S. citizen at the time of application Be at least 18 years old by June 5, 2026 Have a minimum 3.0 GPA on a 4.0 scale Be currently enrolled in or have graduated within the last 2 years Application Deadline June 5, 2026 (11:59 PM ET) Applications are submitted through the ORISE Zintellect system and require: Resume Academic transcript(s) Short essay One recommendation These fellowships provide an exceptional opportunity to gain hands-on federal experience while contributing to nationally significant environmental restoration and modernization efforts. Learn more and apply

Just to make it easier for you here are some vibes: 🧠 The Analyzer — listens to all sides 🔥 The Hot‑Take Machine — strong opinions, zero hesitation 👀 The Observer — watches the debate like a show 🤝 The Peacemaker — tries to understand everyone 🎭 The Switcher — perspective changes depending on the day Which one are you — and why do you think multiple perspectives matter? body: InScribe has a mobile app, available for both iOS and Android ! From the mobile app, you can ask and answer questions and you’ll get notifications about new activity in your community. For detailed information on the features available in the mobile app, check out this resource . 1. Download the Mobile App Search for "InScribe Education" in the Android or IOs app store. You'll sign into the app using a "Sign In Code." This code can be generated in your community when using InScribe from a browser - like Chrome, Safari, or Firefox. To generate the code, just follow these steps: 2. Go to your community through a web browser, the way you normally do. 3. Click on your profile circle in the upper right corner to open the menu, then select, "Mobile App Access" 4. Click the Generate Sign In Code" button. Your code will appear in the gray box to the right of the button. Your sign in code expires after 10 minutes and can only be used one time. If it expires before you can use it, you can click the blue button to generate a new one. Keep your sign in code private because it's connected to your personal InScribe account! 5. Enter this code in the box on the "Mobile App Sign-In" Page and click the "Sign in" button.

title: DeepLearning.AI Prior Learning Credit Opportunity
body: We’re excited to announce that you can now earn 3 elective credits toward your MS-DS degree by completing the DeepLearning.AI Specialization on Coursera through Prior Learning Credit (PLC)! Credits will count as 3 outside electives (up to 6 allowed), but will not affect your GPA. Please note: The specialization is not included in CU on Coursera or Coursera Plus. title: Job Post: Technical Fellow, Wildfire Commercial Solutions
body: The NSF ASCEND Engine is harnessing the unique environmental data assets of the Colorado–Wyoming region to promote place-based innovation for community resilience. The Technical Fellow is a full-time, embedded role focused on wildfire resilience, applied AI, and commercialization. This fellow will work closely with an ASCEND Engine portfolio project, startups, and utility partners, serving as a translational bridge between research outputs and operational deployment. The position is designed to provide technical, commercialization, and pilot-coordination capacity, with an emphasis on accelerating adoption of AI-enabled wildfire resilience tools by utilities and industry partners. The fellow will support the development and execution of pilot projects with ASCEND utility partners, help establish initial platform environments and data pipelines, and coordinate partner engagement to ensure solutions are operator-ready, explainable, and aligned with regulatory, procurement, and operational requirements. Apply here

title: Hey everyone! Stats pathway done + a few questions on pacing, balance, and course updates
body: Hello everyone! Just wanted to pop in and officially say hi to the overall MS-DS cohorts here on iNSCRIBE. I come from a quant background and I just wrapped up the 3-course Stats pathway. Right now, I'm waiting for official admission so I can finally enroll and decide on the curriculum to dive in first. While I'm waiting, I’ve been wondering about a few things and wanted to see what your experiences have been. First off, how are you all handling the pacing? Since there are two sessions per semester, how many courses are you usually tackling per session? To give some context, I’m dealing with a serious illness right now, so I'm actually not working. It's obviously tough, but the silver lining is that I have the time to fully commit to this degree on my time and terms. And my goal is to finish it as fast as possible. For those of you who are working or have families, I have massive respect for you. How are you managing the balancing act? Juggling family, jobs, the heavy course requirements, and the financial realities (course costs, time, opportunity costs) is no joke. I’d love to hear how you make it work? Also, we all know credit hours don't generally reflect time put in the course? Even though the credits are the same, some subjects clearly demand way more blood, sweat, and tears than others. How do you guys figure out which courses are the "heavy hitters" before you register? Another thing I’ve been thinking about is how ridiculously fast AI, Data Science, and STEM programs and uses of them are evolving right now. It feels almost impossible for academic studies to keep pace with real world uses and programs. I noticed that the case studies in some of the foundational 4 course Data Science classes feel like they're still based on when the program first launched. Because of that, my strategy is actually to hold off on those and the AI electives once they drop until later in my degree, just in case they update the material soon. Is anyone else taking a similar approach? Finally, because I'm a stats person, I couldn't help but crunch some numbers on my recent session. By my count, there were roughly 125 unique students in the Stats courses: 85 students took exactly 1 class: one of [5001, 5002, 5003] 24 students took exactly 2 classes: two of [5001, 5002, 5003] 6 students took exactly 3 classes: all of [5001, 5002, 5003 Since I took all three together, a big shoutout to the 16 of you who shared that exact same cohort with me! But since we all choose our own pace from here on out, I'm super excited to cross paths with the thousands of other students in this program over the coming sessions. Wishing everyone here strength, love, bliss, and immense success as we navigate this! Let me know your thoughts on the course loads below. JOHN K

title: Degree (MSDS) Time Estimator - feedback wanted
body: I built a small tool to estimate how long CU Boulder MSDS courses actually take, and I would appreciate feedback from anyone who wants to try it or just roast the approach. What it does: • Predicts calendar days for a target course based on anchor courses you have already finished • Uses the crowdsourced MSDS review sheet data with a Bayesian update (it pulls data from the google sheets from the highlighted post from r/CUBoulderMSCS) • Adjusts for focus ratio, ADHD profile, and self-rated math/coding/writing skills • Returns uncertainty intervals instead of single number guesses How it works: It converts your anchor course days and hours per week into total study hours, compares that against the posterior hour distribution for each target course (updated from student reviews), and transfers your pace via topic similarity. Full derivation is in MODEL.md if you care about the math. Try it or just read the code Repo: https://github.com/utsapoddar/msds-time-estimator There is a nontechnical guide if you do not want to touch Python, plus a notebook version if you prefer that. How you can help: 1. Feedback: do the predictions match your experience? Are they way off for any course you took? 2. Data: more reviews make the posteriors better. If you have not submitted yours yet: http://tinyurl.com/cu-elective-review Caveats: Some courses still fall back to Coursera published hours. Focus ratio adjustments are heuristic constants, not fitted. No cross validation yet. This is a "help me make it less wrong" project. Let me know what you think. Ps. It currently only works for MSDS for now but since I built it modular, it can be changed to any other degree using the skeleton.

title: How do you cope with impostor syndrome?
body: Hello MSDS students. Merriam‑Webster describes imposter syndrome as “persistent doubt concerning one’s abilities or accomplishments accompanied by the fear of being exposed as a fraud despite evidence of ongoing success.” In my opinion It is a pattern I can interrupt, not a personality trait I am stuck with it. I consider them as a mental habit, not a truth. For more information I found a research paper published by research gate directly relevant to engineering, and research computing communities. (PDF) The Imposter Phenomenon in Higher Education: Incidence and Impact. Share your thoughts how are you coping with imposter syndrome.

author: Kyle O'Brien

title: Why the Brand New CompTIA SecAI+ Certificate Is a Good Pivot for MSDS Candidates
body: secaiplus-deck.pdf Pass CompTIA SecAI+ in A Week.pdf I beleive it was February 17 when CompTIA launched a beta version of a brand new certificate -- CompTIA SecAI+. 19 days later I took it and scored a impressive 806 and passed it. The standard format of it is 50 questions and one must score a minimum of 600 to pass and earn the certificate. Here's my honest assessment, in terms of difficulty level, this one is elementary school compared to the super mature ones like CompTIA Security+ which I also possess. But maybe not for too long. If you take the dive now, you 're most likely to pass, if you have taken the CU ML courses or you are simply knowledgable about ML. There is a tiny little that has to do with Data Mining. And the rest is just fluffy security posturing, no different than general cyber security. It is a fast changing landscape. If you grab it now, it can be effortless. I expect CompTIA to *aggressively* crank up the difficulty level, fairly rapidly. I took it only 19 days after the beta but the version was already version 1. There are a lot of need-to-knows I can not fully explain in this short post, but all the secret is in a book I authroed, in case you're interested. Why bother? MSDS folks are *not* implementers of AI Security. Wrong! Just like developers, QA's are not implementers of IT Security, but if you want a tiny slice of the Federal Governement IT/AI contract, Security+ and SecAI+ are your mandatory admission tickets. Without them, the high paying, cushy and comfy governement job would be out of your reach -- to be eligible to have a SF85 or SF85P (Public Trust) or SF86 (Secret and Top Secret) clearance, you *must* have CompTIA Security + and, pretty soon, SecAI+. The book is here if you want to check it out.

title: AI Use in Curriculum Development Policies
body: Some recent discussions have prompted me to wonder what policies, if any, CU Boulder has regarding AI use disclosure in the development of curriculum materials. I did a search and couldn't find much. This link contains employee guidance for safe AI use and input/output considerations, security considerations, etc. This link is CU Boulder specific, covers some additional concerns like copyright considerations but mostly refers to how faculty and instructors should proceed in terms of setting AI policies with their students. This link is found within some of the FAQs of the previous one, and offers some strategies for integrating for creating AI policies, AI literacy, discussion with students about AI, etc. Under ethical considerations there's a brief statement about transparency: In the unpublished study referenced above, 78% of CU Boulder student respondents reported that they wanted faculty to disclose their use of AI. Disclosing your use of AI starts the reciprocal process of building trust. Modeling behaviors that you’d like to see in the classroom sets a standard of mutual respect and fortifies trust between you and your students. The unpublished study, for anyone interested, can be found here . If anyone knows about official policies and procedures, please point me in that direction! Otherwise, I'm interested to know if anything of this nature is in the works. There is, of course, a lengthy list of resources in terms of student AI use/honor code/syllabus policies etc. etc. etc. and it's possible I'm in the wrong place since those types of resources dominate my searches. For what it's worth, I feel quite strongly that AI use in curriculum should be explicitly noted, especially in the context of an online program where we don't actively interact with course instructors and rely solely on the course materials they have produced and provided. Honestly, an explicit disclosure that AI was not used on courses would also be good for full transparency, and I've seen a number of disclosures like this that AI was not used to create this content recently. Thoughts?
author: Michaela R

title: Colorado Science & Technology Policy Summer Scholars program (STPP Scholars)
body: The Institute for Science & Policy at the Denver Museum of Nature and Science is pleased to announce the launch of the Colorado Science & Technology Policy Summer Scholars program (STPP Scholars). Founded in 2018 by former State Senators Chris Hansen and Bob Rankin as the Colorado Science & Engineering Policy Fellowship, the STPP Scholars program offers the same nonpartisan professional development programming for current Colorado STEM students interested in state-level public policy. Summer Scholars will spend 8 weeks learning about science and technology policy through seminars and site visits, culminating in a capstone policy research project and presentation at the Colorado State Capitol. The 2026 Scholars Program will run from June 15 to August 7, 2026. Scholars are expected to be at the Colorado State Capitol building Monday through Friday, approximately 32 hours per week. Scholars will receive a stipend of $5,000. Scholars will work with legislative sponsors, Senator Cleave Simpson and Representative Sean Camacho, throughout the summer to develop their policy proposals. In addition to the capstone project, Scholars will: Participate in an introductory policy boot camp to learn about the policy-making process at the Capitol and acquire the skills they will need to be a part of it. Engage with stakeholders at site visits across Colorado and gain firsthand experiences learning how technology, science, and policy intersect. Attend seminars and presentations from guest speakers and industry experts. Applications are due by 5 pm MT on Monday, March 16, 2026. CO STPP Summer Scholars One Pager.pdf Email CoSTPP@dmns.org with any questions.

title: Mindfulness Practices for Graduate Students
body: March 31, April 3, 7, 10, 14, 17 from 12 – 12:45 p.m. via zoom Join Kim Stephens, Wardenburg Acupuncturist and Mindfulness Educator, to learn evidence-based mindfulness mediation practices to improve focus and concentration and overall wellbeing. The curriculum is designed to be accessible, practical for everyday life, and supportive of student well-being. It includes a series of 6 virtual sessions, offered twice weekly for 3 weeks. Weekly guided lessons focused on evidence-based mindfulness practices that support stress reduction, concentration, and emotional balance. Each lesson is designed to be about 45 minutes long and includes education regarding the purpose of the practice, the science behind the practice and time to practice each tool as a group. Short practices students can integrate into their daily routines, such as mindful breathing, mindful movement, managing thoughts and emotions, body awareness practices for releasing tension, stress, and practices that support mood and connection. Community and Connection Building: We will explore practices that have been proven to promote greater empathy, compassion, and connection. The program is best learned as a series but is also designed for students to drop in to individual sessions. Session 1: Mindful Awareness for Focus and Attention Tuesday, March 31, 12:00-12:45 p.m. Introduction to mindfulness, attention training and the importance of being present. Learn how to train the mind without judgment. Key practices: simple awareness exercises, mindful listening, attention games. Session 2: Mindfulness of Breathing Friday, April 3, 12:00-12:45 p.m. Discover how to use the breath as an anchor to steady and calm the wandering mind. Key practices: Breath awareness, anchor breathing and counting breaths. Session 3: Understanding the Brain and Stress Response / Mindful Behavior vs. Autopilot Behavior Tuesday, April 7, 12:00-12:45 p.m. Learn the neuroscience behind stress, fight-flight-freeze response and how mindfulness techniques help calm and reset. Key practices: Stress mapping, journaling stress triggers and CALM breathing when activated. Session 4: Noticing Body Sensations: Release Tension for Reduced Stress Friday, April 10, 12:00-12:45 p.m. Understand why noticing body sensations matters and how it helps release tension. Key practices: Body scan, mindful movement and tense-release exercises. Session 5: Noticing Our Thoughts and Emotions Tuesday, April 14, 12:00-12:45 p.m. Practice observing thoughts, noticing emotions without judgment and negative self-talk patterns and how mindfulness helps. Key practices: Thought labeling, noticing body sensations associated with different emotions and RAIN technique. Session 6: Hardwiring Our Brain for Positivity and Well-being / Mindful Self-Compassion Friday, April 17, 12:00-12:45 p.m. Learn how gratitude, generosity, and kind thoughts strengthen neural pathways for well-being. In times of difficulty, learn to cultivate kindness towards self. Key practices: 5 finger gratitude, loving kindness meditation and mindful self-compassion. Sign up here for any or all sessions!

body: The University of Colorado Boulder Counseling & Psychiatric Services (CAPS) is excited to share that BetterMynd , a third-party telehealth therapy platform, is officially live and available to students now! CU is expanding mental health support options for our students. This free, flexible, and confidential online counseling platform is designed specifically for college students and is available immediately. Key Things to Know Open to all students with an active IdentiKey Therapy services only — psychiatry and medication management are not available through BetterMynd. Students receive 7 free 50-minute sessions to start, with the option to request additional sessions if clinically appropriate. No insurance is required. What is BetterMynd? Through BetterMynd, students can access secure tele-therapy sessions with licensed mental health professionals from wherever they feel most comfortable. Therapists can support students with concerns such as: Stress, anxiety, or burnout Relationship or roommate challenges Identity-related concerns Depression or feeling stuck Family stressors Academic or life transitions Why Students May Choose BetterMynd Flexible scheduling – Daytime, evening, weekend, and break availability Meet from anywhere – Laptop, phone, or tablet; no travel required Diverse licensed counselors – Students can browse therapists by identities, lived experience, and specialty areas Confidential and secure – Designed to provide private, judgment-free support How Students Get Started Visit bettermynd.com Register using their CU Boulder IdentiKey Complete a brief intake form Browse therapist profiles and schedule a free 50-minute session Attend sessions virtually via Zoom from any device

title: Has anyone had to use Honorlock for their final exams?
body: I'm a full time student and just started taking DTSA 5504 Data Mining Pipeline a few days ago. As an aside, it was a very introductory course and a little bit disappointing. Anyway... I just finished the content and was going to take my final exam today. However, the university is running a pilot for a new proctoring service (instead of ProctorU) which requires the addition of a chrome extension called Honorlock. The benefit of this new service is that you no longer have to schedule your exams 72 hours in advance because Honorlock uses AI to proctor. All sounded good until I went to download the extension. First noticed it has 1.1/5 stars with over 4k reviews. All are students with mostly general complaints but some complaints are particularly concerning with students commenting that their systems ended up with malware. I also checked the archived channel on Slack for DTSA 5504 and a couple students from Fall 2 2025 had posted about technical issues with trying to take their exams. So this had me pretty concerned but I finally got the courage to hit the "add to chrome" download button for the extension because I'd really prefer to take the exam this session. Well, before downloading you get one final warning message (thank god): "Add Honorlock? It can: read and change all data on all websites read and change your browsing history on all your signed-in devices capture content of your screen change and grant access to features such as geolocation, microphone, camera, cookies, etc. for all your websites and extensions, including this extension. manage your apps, extensions, and themes" Holy... total loss for words on this. The University has been running this pilot for a full session already and they are still doing it? I mean, I guess I just can't complete the course this session. Has anyone tried using Honorlock or run into this roadblock like I have? Curious what other CU Boulder students are thinking. Oh, and thank you all for listening to this rant. Edit: I ended up creating a junk gmail account and started a fresh chrome browsing session with this account. Doubled checked the status of my cache/cookies on chrome to confirm it was new and not connected to my usual chrome browsing account. I then installed Honorlock, took the exam (no issues!), and uninstalled the extension after completing the exam.

title: Cracking LeetCode and Data Structure Interviews in FAANG
body: I wanted to start a discussion about LeetCode and data structure questions that are asked in FAANG interviews and other company interviews. No matter what level you are at if you are applying for technical roles you usually have to clear 3 to 4 interview rounds. A lot of those rounds include coding questions, especially data structures and algorithms. That is why I think this topic is important for all of us. One thing that makes interview preparation hard is that LeetCode has so many questions. There are around 1500 questions, so it is not possible to memorize everything. But at the same time, many questions follow the same patterns. The patterns repeat, but each question still has its own trick and that is what makes interviews challenging. My personal way of handling these questions is spaced repetition. I believe it is important to keep reviewing algorithms, patterns, and problem-solving tricks again and again over time. When I practice regularly, it becomes easier to recall the right approach during an interview. I also think it helps to connect these ideas with daily practice, projects, or even problem-solving in your job. I want to know how other people are preparing for these interviews. How are you planning to tackle LeetCode or data structure questions? Are you focusing on solving many questions, or are you focusing more on understanding patterns deeply? What methods are helping you stay consistent and remember the tricks? I would love to hear your opinions and learn about the strategies that are working for you.

title: Anyone Taking "Introduction to Machine Learning: Unsupervised Learning" for Spring One 2026
body: It appears the course design has some annoying glitches. 1)Throughout labs in the five modules, lab chanllenges/questions are using customtomized version of "usarrest.csv", "wine.csv", "ratings.csv", "iris.csv", "rating_ground_truth.csv" -- they are not the generic version of dataset you download by using the Python sklearn library. That is all cool but it appears to me that the course designers depended on AI to generate hidden test cells for the Jupyter Notebook lab assginement. For example, one of the hidden assertion test was hard coded to check against the generic version of csv's you download via sklearn instead of the csv specifically placed in the lab path. Each lab has a "setup" section that we are not supposed to alter and it reads the csv from the preset path, while the hidden asserts were actually checking against the non-customized version. This is pure evil. It literally wasted 1-2 hours of my life poking around and pulling my hair out before I figured out it is a fatal error on the lab design. Additionally, some of the "free text" entries under quizes strikes as myterious -- and makes me think that the quiz design is absolutely faulty and sloppy. Want an example? Look here--https://www.coursera.org/learn/unsupervised-algorithms-in-machine-learning/discussions/weeks/5/threads/wfxvAxAgEfGgMhIihID3kQ?newThread=true Has there been any basic QA on this course's design and release? I wonder.

title: Calling 2026-2027 DS Mentors!
body: It's that time of year to recruit our next cohort of mentors! As you know, we welcome new students to the program every 8 weeks, and often times students can feel isolated in a fully online program. To support new students, we launched the Data Science Mentorship Program for Coursera students a year ago, and are now seeking to hire 2026-2027 mentors! If you are interested in becoming a mentor to new MS-DS students, please complete the survey by March 8th at 11:59 p.m. MT. What is a MS-DS Peer Mentor? MS-DS Mentors act as student ambassadors for the program and welcome new MS-DS students to CU-Boulder. This offers students a chance to grow their leadership and communication skills and inspire other students to take a more active role in representing the program. The role of a peer mentor is to provide support, encouragement, and information. Peer mentors serve as graduate student life experts and they may have suggestions about work-life balance, adjusting to life as a graduate student, advisor/advisee relationships, etc. We hope that out of this program, both new and seasoned students can make lifetime connections, learn more about the program, and gain a sense of community. We’re looking for current MS-DS Students who are… Excited to welcome new MS-DS Students. Happy to answer new student questions. Committed to helping incoming students have a great experience. Enthusiastic representatives of the program. Reliable and independent. In good academic standing with a 3.0 GPA or higher. At least 10 credits into the program. Admitted to the MS-DS on Coursera program Planning to graduate in a year or more. Mentor Responsibilities: Encourage and empower personal development of students. Field general questions about your experience in the program and provide suggestions based on your experience. Develop and maintain a broader perspective on career options and opportunities. Make a genuine commitment to mentoring relationships. Lead and participate in MS-DS mentorship socials on Zoom regularly. Participate in InScribe as a moderator, which entails posing new conversations and responding to student questions. Opportunities to participate in alumni mentorship events such as alumni panels. What’s in it for you? Meet new people and make friends. Help maintain a welcoming environment for new MS-DS Students. Improve your leadership and communication skills. Add leadership and mentorship skills to your resume. Receive Data Science swag. Receive a $250/year stipend. APPLY TO BE A MENTOR

title: Everybody talks about shipping AI products but not many people explain how to actually do it.
body: Everybody talks about shipping AI products but not many people explain how to actually do it. If you want a solid, practical path, these 5 books will take you a long way : 1) Designing Machine Learning Systems — Chip Huyen https://www.amazon.com/Designing-Machine-Learning-Systems-Production-Ready/dp/1098107969 One of the best beginner-friendly books on designing ML systems. It builds strong intuition for what an end-to-end ML system looks like and how the pieces fit together. 2) Prompt Engineering for LLMs — John Berryman & Albert Ziegler https://www.amazon.com/Prompt-Engineering-LLMs-Model-Based-Applications/dp/1098156153 If you like thinking in systems and want to understand how to get consistent results from LLMs, this is a great read. It teaches how to design prompts that are flexible, scalable, and effective. 3) AI Engineering — Chip Huyen https://www.amazon.com/AI-Engineering-Building-Applications-Foundation/dp/1098166302 Clear explanations of: AI Engineering vs. ML Engineering RAG (from classic search to more semantic systems) Building agentic systems LLMOps (especially observability and user feedback) 4) Building LLMs for Production — Louis-François Bouchard & Louie Peters https://academy.towardsai.net/courses/buildingllmsforproduction A hands-on guide to implementing core techniques used in real LLM apps. It uses LangChain and LlamaIndex to build different RAG approaches, starting from the basics. Great for entry-to-mid-level engineers. 5) LLMs in Production — Chris Brousseau & Matthew Sharp https://www.amazon.com/LLMs-Production-language-successful-products/dp/1633437205 This one is fully focused on shipping. It covers topics like: Optimizing LLMs with practical tools Data engineering for LLM applications Training and serving at scale Infrastructure considerations for production systems How many of these have you read?

title: Data for Social Justice Speaker Series: A Case for Quantitative Justice
body: Passing along an event hosted by CU Boulder's Computer Science department. Data Science students, please feel free to join if this event interests you! Speaker: Ranthony Clark (Duke) - a rising star in mathematics and algorithmic fairness Title: A Case for Quantitative Justice Abstract: Quantitative Justice is an emerging interdisciplinary field that asks a simple but powerful question: How can the mathematical sciences engage with society to uncover and address inequities? Drawing on ideas from mathematics, statistics, computer science, and data science, researchers in this field develop rigorous tools to study real-world systems that shape people’s lives. In this talk, I will introduce the central ideas of Quantitative Justice and highlight examples from research communities where quantitative approaches illuminate questions of fairness and representation. I will focus on my own work in voting rights and electoral redistricting as a case study, showing how data-driven methods can deepen our understanding of democratic processes and reveal structural inequalities. Along the way, we’ll explore how to approach social questions from a quantitative lens: framing problems, designing analyses, and interpreting results in ways that are both mathematically rich and socially meaningful. I will also reflect on my path into this field, and on the opportunities it offers for students and scholars interested in using data and computation in service of a better society. Bio: Ranthony A. Clark is a National Science Foundation Ascending Postdoctoral Fellow, Phillip Griffiths Assistant Research Professor, and Computational and Mathematical Science Fellow for the Center for Computational Thinking at Duke University. She earned a PhD in Mathematics in 2018 from the University of Iowa, an MS from Eastern Kentucky University in 2013, and a BS in Math and a BA in English from the University of Kentucky in 2011. Her research interests include applied algebraic topology, data science, commutative ring theory, math education, and the history of Black mathematicians. Dr. Clark is deeply invested in quantitative justice, that is, using mathematical tools to address societal issues rooted in inequity. Her current work in quantitative justice involves applications of mathematics and data science to electoral redistricting. She was a Berlekamp Postdoctoral Fellow for the Fall 2023 Semester program on Algorithms, Fairness, and Equity at the Simons Laufer Mathematical Sciences Institute (SLMath) and currently works with the Quantitative Gerrymandering Group in the Department of Mathematics at Duke University.

body: Are you close to graduating? Look here to see information about degree conferral and commencement. Graduation Timeline You do not need to apply to graduate. Degree conferrals are processed automatically for students who have met the degree requirements as outlined on the Special Programs section of the University Catalog. Students who have met the degree requirements will receive an email confirming their degree conferral about 3-4 weeks after classes end for the session (See the calendar for exact dates). Diploma We'll mail your complimentary paper diploma and/or certificate approximately eight weeks after the end of the session in which you complete your degree requirements. You should verify both your name that will appear on your diploma, as well as the address your diploma will be mailed to in your Buff Portal . You will need to Update Your Contact Information if your name and/or home address are not listed correctly. You also have access to a Certified Electronic Diploma & Certificate (CeDiplomas/CeCertificates). Go to the electronic diplomas & certificates card in Buff Portal and click the "Access your CeDiploma/CeCertificate" link to access CeCredential Trust, an approved third-party vendor of CU Boulder. Please see CeDiplomas/CeCertificates for additional information regarding electronic diplomas. Transcripts Before ordering the official CU Boulder transcript, students must check for holds by logging into the Buff Portal . If there are financial obligations with CU, the student transcript will not be released. Order Transcripts Here Note : Click on the "Students with an IdentiKey" button to start ordering an official transcript. Commencement You are welcome to attend the graduation ceremony on-campus upon completion of your MS-DS degree. There is one campuswide ceremony per year and one Data Science Graduation Recognition Ceremony per year, both held at the end of the Spring 2. Information regarding the graduation ceremonies will be sent to students who have met all of the graduation requirements. Check your CU student email! For more information about campus wide Commencement, please visit the Commencement website For more information about the Data Science Graduation Recognition Ceremony, please visit the Graduation Information page . Please note that we do not issue visas for international students wishing to attend the in-person graduation ceremony. Regalia Information In order to walk in the commencement ceremony, students must wear approved regalia. Master’s students should wear regalia purchased online from Jostens . The regalia set includes the gown, hood, cap/tam, tassel and is yours to keep. You can place your order for the ‘Masters Cap, Gown, Hood, Tassel w/ Yeardate Charm’ ($48.60) directly on Jostens website and it will be shipped directly to your home.Please select ‘Engineering’ as under the ‘Major/Curriculum’ since you will be receiving a Master of Science degree. If you are an international student interested in shipping directly to the Data Science Office, please reach out to msds-support@colorado.edu for the address. Buffs Alumni Association Now that you are a graduate of the University of Colorado Boulder, you are welcome to join the Buffs Alumni Association, known as Forever Buffs. Please see the Forever Buffs website for more information on joining the association. More Questions? Please refer to the Master of Data Sciences Graduation Information page and Current Students page.

Look here for more information on MSDS program admissions requirements and process. Prerequisite Knowledge There are no formal prerequisites for the MS-DS on Coursera. However, students should be knowledgeable in the following: Python R programming Calculus including derivatives and integrals Linear algebra including matrix multiplication, matrix inversion, and solving linear systems using matrices Statistics If you would like to brush up on the above skills before starting the program, consider the following classes on Coursera: Expressway to Data Science: Essential Math Specialization Calculus: Algebra and Differential Calculus for Data Science Linear Algebra: Essential Linear Algebra for Data Science Integral Calculus: Integral Calculus and Numerical Analysis for Data Science R Programming: R Programming & Tidyverse Specialization Python: Python Programming Specialization Statistics: Statistics and Probability ( Khan Academy ) Admissions Requirements Simply complete a pathway specialization to demonstrate your proficiency and be admitted to the program. Students are automatically admitted to the degree program after meeting all admission requirements below. All admitted students receive an official offer letter via email. See the MS-DS on Coursera Student Handbook for details. Pass one pathway with a pathway GPA of 3.0 or higher Earn a C or better in all pathway courses within your chosen pathway Earn an overall cumulative GPA of 3.0 or higher Indicate interest in degree admission (via the enrollment form) A pathway specialization (or "pathway") is a series of three 1-credit courses with a focus on either statistics or computer science. The credits you earn for pathway courses are part of the required curriculum, so you make direct progress toward your degree as you complete your pathway. Choose one of the following pathways: Statistics Pathway Data Science Foundations: Statistical Inference DTSA 5001: Probability Theory: Applications for Data Science DTSA 5002: Statistical Inference for Estimation in Data Science DTSA 5003: Hypothesis Testing for Data Science Computer Science Pathway Data Science Foundations: Data Structures and Algorithms DTSA 5501: Algorithms for Searching, Sorting & Indexing DTSA 5502: Trees & Graphs: Basics DTSA 5503: Dynamic Programming, Greedy Algorithms *Please note that both pathways are required to meet degree requirements. Pick one to attempt admission. Converting Grades Converting Percentage (%) to Letter Grade (A–F) You can find the grading breakdown for each course on Coursera. Simply go to the course in question, find the Reading: Syllabus item (usually in Week 1), and then scroll down to the section outlining the uniform letter grade rubric for that class. Converting Letter Grades (A–F) to the 4.0 Scale You can convert letter grades to the 4.0 scale with the CU Transcript Key . Note that the Numeric Grades (Law) column there only applies to Law School classes and is unrelated to the MS-DS. Admissions Timeline Newly admitted students typically receive an email confirming their degree admission about 2–3 weeks after classes end for the session . (See the calendar for exact dates.) Please note that admissions for Spring 1 sessions are typically delayed by a few weeks due to the winter break. If you believe you are eligible for admission to the MS-DS degree, please... See Admissions to confirm you meet all requirements. Watch for an admissions confirmation email within a few weeks after the end of the session. If you still have not received an admissions email 3 weeks after the end of the session, please send an email from your colorado.edu email to msds-support@coursera.org for support. All students must complete a short, non-credit Community Equity course in Canvas after admission to the degree program. See Step 7: Complete Community Equity Course under Getting Started above for details. More Questions? Please refer to the Master of Data Sciences Admissions page, FAQ page and Current Students page.

title: Philosophy of Hypothesis Testing (naturalistic)
body: Hi everyone, hope everyone is good. I couldn't help but think that statistical hypothesis testing almost represents a way of thinking about how one interacts with the world. We perceive things (through our senses) and in those perceptions we "measure" or "locate" or "register" the "intensity" (both the magnitude of means, let's say, or of minima, or of maxima, and of a presentation of variation) of those occurrences. We also project ourselves back onto those occurrences by changing them or making decisions in respect of them. eg. I perceive dirty dishes and then proceed to clean them with a sponge and some cleaning agent. But my perception of "how dirty" those dishes are is an immediate statistical measurement. it is a sample. I don't go and count how many specks or crumbs are on the dish; I simply perceive something like a sample, gauge its relationship with the distribution of dirt that I hold probably exists on the plate, and then i decide how much effort cleaning that particular dish requires. It might seem a simple example. But let's say that I have 100 dishes to clean. Then if I apply a simple enough rule, if i can find one, based on maybe a simple enough test, I can achieve efficiencies at scale. It's all very mysterious and interesting to me, and i haven't figured the full picture out, but there is this sense that there is some kind of natural basis or natural cognition occurring in hypothesis tests, which themselves are part of or should be part of "designs" or even, dare one say, "narratives". In some sense, certain hypothesis tests are doomed to fail to reject because the alternative is misspecified, or the distributional assumptions do not correspond with the data enough, and these become closer to "fictions". But the varieties are many. It's all very interesting and mysterious. Lol. I can't help but speculate that there are kinds of statistical hypothesis testing processes going on all the time throughout Nature, with absolutely no "trained" statistician around.

channel: Data Science Foundations: Data Structures and Algorithms
title: Transition guide for using CLRS 4th edition vs 3rd edition
body: For the course textbook, the data structures and algorithms pathway recommends using the 3rd edition of Introduction to Algorithms by Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, and Stein (CLRS). However, if interested, the 4th edition of CLRS is also available and accessible online through the CU Boulder library catalog here . For those using CLRS 4th edition, the "3e to 4e Transition Guide" on the CLRS webpage at MIT Press (found under the "Resources" tab here , among other useful links) provides a nice summary of the global, organizational, and content changes that were made in the 4th edition. For one, I found this helpful in mapping the course reading assignments to the updated chapter numbering in the 4th edition.

title: DTSA 5511 Lab 2 - Workaround for Q2/Q9 conflict
body: In 5511 Lab 2, the Fashion-MNIST dataset is split into two files (60K training/10K test). I ran into a conflict between Q2 and Q9 in the autograder: Using only the 60K training file for the train/val/test split passes Q2 (which has 60K hardcoded) but seems to fails Q9's tests on model accuracy for the test set Combining both files (70K total) before splitting gives better model performance but fails Q2's size check A workaround if you hit this issue: Use the 60K training file only and split it in Q2 to pass the autograder Immediately after Q2, recombine the training and test files (70K total) and re-split for train/val/test Use this 70K split for all subsequent model training (Q3-Q9) This passed the check in Q2 and gave the models enough accuracy to pass Q9.

title: DTSA 5734 - Exporting queries/answersets from Docker VM
body: Hello! I have just completed the final assignment for this course and want to share with others one trick I have discovered through the process. I saw many students wonder how they can export their work from Docker VM provided in the course to their own computers. While many students use a workaround of screenshoting their work, there is one more option I want to share with others. Namely, in PGadmin you can save your queries as .sql file and export answer sets as .csv files. VM has the access to internet, so you can use it to send those files back to your own computer. I particilarly found send-anywhere.com useful for the purpose. Hope this helps future students.
author: Bojan Jovanovic

title: DTSA 5734—Creating a local copy of the course databases
body: Hello, All! I started the course recently and was finding the PostgreSQL instance in the Coursera lab environment problematic to use for various reasons (network lag, non-persistent environment, no direct copying/pasting between Docker VM and local machine, etc.). I ended up installing PostgreSQL locally with exported copies of the Northwinds and ClassicModels databases, and, so far, have found this setup much easier to use to complete the labs. I wanted to share this with folks who might be having similar difficulties: I found the instructions in this GitHub README , from this archived Slack channel post , a great help in exporting the necessary databases from the lab environment and getting everything set up on my local machine. These instructions were originally posted in Slack in December 2023, but they're still relevant and helpful today. A big thanks to @Joseph Leakakos, if he's still around in the MS-DS program, for taking the time to put this together!

channel: Data Science Methods for Quality Improvement
title: Tips for Installing Required R Libraries in DTSA 5704
body: I recently started DTSA 5704: Managing, Describing, and Analyzing Data, and I’m currently in Week 2 of Course 1. I wanted to share something that might help others who are just getting started. To complete the assignments, you’ll need to install the following R libraries, as shown in the course videos: install.packages("devtools") require(devtools) install_github("burrm/lolcat") I initially ran into errors when trying to install these packages. What solved the issue for me was uninstalling R completely and then reinstalling it. After that, everything worked without any problems. I thought I’d share this in case anyone else encounters the same issue. A bit about me: English is not my first language, and my previous degree was completed in Japanese. Fortunately, the instructor speaks clearly and at a comfortable pace, so the lectures have been easy to follow. I currently work in manufacturing, focusing on data analysis and automation, and I was excited to see datasets in the course that are similar to what I use at work. I usually work with Python, but I’m finding that R can be surprisingly concise for certain tasks.

author: Trey H

title: Review of Courses 1 and 2 (and Plans for Course 3)
body: I am writing this post to help you decide whether these three courses might be a good fit for you. At the moment, I have completed all parts of Course 1 except for the final exam, and I am currently working through Course 2. I have not taken Course 3 yet, so I plan to review it later. This course does not include programming assignments or coding exams; the grade is based solely on quizzes. Since computer vision processes images as RGB numerical matrices, some background knowledge in linear algebra is required. The course includes hands-on exercises in Excel while watching the lecture videos. I found it interesting because adjusting the parameters allows you to visually observe how the output changes, which really clicked for me. However, some of the custom Excel functions used in the course did not work in my local Excel 2019 environment. I solved this by uploading the files to the Microsoft 365 account provided by the university and completing the exercises there. Course 1 covers topics such as image transformations, edge detection, and feature extraction. Course 2 focuses on deep learning, and I was able to deepen my understanding of layers, activation functions, and other core concepts. If you are new to deep learning, it might be helpful to take this course alongside—or even before—the deep learning section of Course 3 in the machine learning courses, as it could make the learning process smoother.
